text: The surgeon applies an ICG tattoo at the distal black ink tattoo site, located one centimeter distal to the tumor at the level of the anorectal ring.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to isolate the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a sponge to keep the small bowel out of the surgical field, accommodating the patient's size during the procedure.

The surgeon frequently repositions the surgical field for optimal visualization, revealing the ureter as they proceed with a medial to lateral dissection towards the sidewall, approaching the superior hemorrhoidal vessel just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal vein, located adjacent to the artery, just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, which has been isolated during the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon sweeps the nerves posteriorly, maintaining the dissection plane against the glistening yellow mesorectum, with visible edema present in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection circumferentially, working down the right side and preparing to move anteriorly, maintaining exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the proximal black tattoo, located a few centimeters above the lesion, ensuring avoidance of the vagina during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a left lateral dissection to access the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully maneuvering through the tissue.
The surgeon joins the medially dissected plane and proceeds to dissect down the left-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, maintaining careful separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon continues the dissection down the left-hand side, with clear visualization of the surgical plane. The left ureter is identified and preserved as the surgeon joins the previously dissected anterior plane.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, carefully avoiding the vagina while ensuring thorough dissection of the anterior mesorectum. The dissection follows the crackly plane, visibly joining circumferentially with the previously dissected areas.
The surgeon navigates the dissection plane, using visible nerves as landmarks to avoid drifting too posteriorly towards the sacrum, ensuring the dissection remains in the proper anatomical plane.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the rectum, strategically working in areas with easier access to improve retraction and facilitate further dissection in other directions.

The surgeon uses arm number one to push up against the vagina, working within the defined plane while searching for ICG tattoos. The tattoos are not clearly visible yet, indicating the need to continue dissection distally.

The surgeon navigates the crackly plane posteriorly, then shifts focus to the left side where less dissection has been performed. The visibility improves significantly after completing the posterior and anterior dissections.
The surgeon moves anteriorly and identifies a black tattoo marking on the colon, which is noted to be one centimeter distal to the tumor as per the original colonoscopy findings.
The surgeon identifies an ICG tattoo distal to the previously seen black tattoo, visible within the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon continues to dissect distally, attempting to keep the yellow fat adhered to the rectum. Blunt dissection is utilized to aid in this process.
The surgeon reaches the level below the distal extent of the anterior mesorectum, revealing the rectum adjacent to the vagina as the mesorectum disappears.
The surgeon continues to search for the ICG tattoo in the surgical field, while navigating through areas of intense radiation fibrosis, likely due to tumor regression.
The surgical view reveals the area below the distal extent of the posterior mesorectum, with the levator muscles visible in the field.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the mesorectum, ensuring not to cone in on it, while maintaining the integrity of the mesorectum with the specimen.

The surgeon identifies a small tattoo on the patient's right side and switches to a vessel sealer for improved control and hemostasis. The instrument is used to manage blood vessels in the lower surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking on the tissue, indicating the desired dissection level. The mesorectum appears mostly freed circumferentially, with some remaining tissue still attached.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue off the levators, using precise maneuvers to separate the mesorectum. To reach further distal areas, the surgeon switches back to using scissors for enhanced control.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision (TME), dissecting onto the peritoneal body. The right-sided tattoo is visible, marking the area for dissection.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the mesorectum, revealing the bare rectal wall. The surgical field is prepared for stapling beneath the exposed area.

The surgeon ensures the remaining mesorectum is circumferentially freed to prevent it from being caught in the stapler. The distal stapling is prepared using a trocar in arm number three, with a quick posterior check revealing the unicoccygeal ligament extending into the levators.

The surgeon has repositioned the camera to port two and the stapler to port three, achieving a straight shot for stapling. The stapler is positioned vertically from anterior to posterior. The surgeon ensures the stapler is below the tattoo mark, carefully avoiding the vagina, before firing the stapler.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, examining the pelvic cavity to assess the final outcome. The stapling process may require multiple applications to avoid jagged edges, and the surgeon considers options for mucosectomy and hand-sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the coloanal anastomosis and creates a diverting loop ileostomy.
